## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Intricate test-infrastructure refactor carrying a premise correction: the prior docket had ownership backwards. Rust authors fixtures (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry, fixture structs naming themselves); bash only holds redundant copies in its suite arrays. This is not a bash→Rust projection — it is a consolidation of test vocabulary into its rightful owner. Touches the spine of every `rbw-ts` run, so verification is behavioral, not just a green build.
  
  ## Goal
  
  Make theurge (Rust) the sole owner of test vocabulary: relocate suite definitions from bash into theurge, and collapse fixture-name identity onto Rust identifiers — retiring every hand-maintained name string on both sides of the boundary.
  
  ## Shape
  
  One end state ("Rust owns what fixtures exist, how they group, and what they're named"), reached along two strands:
  
  - **Suites into theurge.** The `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays and `zrbte_resolve_suite` in `rbte_engine.sh` are the redundant copy of Rust-owned fixture names. Move suite definitions into a new Rust module (minted prefix `rbtdrs`, "suites"). A suite is compile-checked **fixture refs** (the statics `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` already holds), never name strings. `rbte_suite` collapses to: build binary, forward the suite name; the binary grows a `suite` dispatch mode beside its existing `single` mode. Each suite carries a human description + optional precondition **printed at runtime** at suite start, so a failure shows its context — superseding the buried bash precondition comments.
  
  - **Fixture identity = Rust identifier.** Retire the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab consts; a fixture's name derives from its Rust identifier (the `case!` macro in `rbtdre_engine.rs` is the stringify precedent). Repoint every consumer (`grep RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`) and `rbtdrm_required_colophons` onto the derived names. Operator wire-names for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` shift kebab→identifier; accepted, since individual fixture runs are rare.
  
  ## Locked constraints
  
  - **Zero new crates.** The suite registry and name derivation use native `macro_rules!` only — no `paste`, no concat crate. Derive names via `stringify!`; express composition as base-suite + extras concatenated at resolve time, so a base like `fast` stays single-source (no flat re-enumeration, no multi-place edits).
  - **Calibrants stay out of `rbw-ts` suites.** Their cases intentionally Fail/Skip to calibrate the harness, asserted under `rbw-tt` (`rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`). They are documented, not suited.
  - **Navigational context is part of done.** Update the CLAUDE.md "Test Execution" section and `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-theurge-ifrit-context.md` so a restarted chat navigates the new model without rediscovery — including a definition of "calibrant" and the `rbw-tt` vs `rbw-ts` split.
  
  ## Sources
  
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — suite arrays + `rbte_suite` (→ forwarder)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` — `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry + lookup
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdre_engine.rs` — `Fixture` struct + `case!` stringify precedent
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` — binary dispatch (add `suite` mode)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` hand-mirror + `rbtdrm_required_colophons`
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` — calibrant meta-tests (held separate)
  
  ## What done looks like
  
  `rbw-ts fast` runs the identical fixture set in identical order as today, and every suite name resolves to the membership it has now. No `ZRBTE_SUITE_*`/`zrbte_resolve_suite` in bash; no `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` strings anywhere. `rbw-tb` builds deny-warnings clean, `rbw-tt` green (calibrants included), `rbw-tq` clean. CLAUDE.md and the theurge context file describe the new test model and define "calibrant."

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BP
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.
6	
7	The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.
8	
9	## Locked decisions
10	
11	- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
12	- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
13	- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All four co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
14	- **Co-maintained constant set in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earn a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.
15	- **Fixture names are Rust-authored — out of the projection.** On inspection, fixtures are defined in Rust (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry; fixture structs name themselves), and bash holds only redundant copies in its `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` suite arrays — there is no canonical bash author, so fixtures fail the single-author rule. The redundancy is removed in the other direction: suite definitions relocate into theurge (Rust) and bash becomes a pure forwarder. This is an ownership relocation, not a bash→Rust constant projection — a future pace must not re-attempt projecting fixture names.
16	
17	## What done looks like
18	
19	The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).
20	
21	# jjezs_pace ₢BPAAG
22	
23	## Character
24	
25	Intricate test-infrastructure refactor carrying a premise correction: the prior docket had ownership backwards. Rust authors fixtures (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry, fixture structs naming themselves); bash only holds redundant copies in its suite arrays. This is not a bash→Rust projection — it is a consolidation of test vocabulary into its rightful owner. Touches the spine of every `rbw-ts` run, so verification is behavioral, not just a green build.
26	
27	## Goal
28	
29	Make theurge (Rust) the sole owner of test vocabulary: relocate suite definitions from bash into theurge, and collapse fixture-name identity onto Rust identifiers — retiring every hand-maintained name string on both sides of the boundary.
30	
31	## Shape
32	
33	One end state ("Rust owns what fixtures exist, how they group, and what they're named"), reached along two strands:
34	
35	- **Suites into theurge.** The `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays and `zrbte_resolve_suite` in `rbte_engine.sh` are the redundant copy of Rust-owned fixture names. Move suite definitions into a new Rust module (minted prefix `rbtdrs`, "suites"). A suite is compile-checked **fixture refs** (the statics `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` already holds), never name strings. `rbte_suite` collapses to: build binary, forward the suite name; the binary grows a `suite` dispatch mode beside its existing `single` mode. Each suite carries a human description + optional precondition **printed at runtime** at suite start, so a failure shows its context — superseding the buried bash precondition comments.
36	
37	- **Fixture identity = Rust identifier.** Retire the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab consts; a fixture's name derives from its Rust identifier (the `case!` macro in `rbtdre_engine.rs` is the stringify precedent). Repoint every consumer (`grep RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`) and `rbtdrm_required_colophons` onto the derived names. Operator wire-names for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` shift kebab→identifier; accepted, since individual fixture runs are rare.
38	
39	## Locked constraints
40	
41	- **Zero new crates.** The suite registry and name derivation use native `macro_rules!` only — no `paste`, no concat crate. Derive names via `stringify!`; express composition as base-suite + extras concatenated at resolve time, so a base like `fast` stays single-source (no flat re-enumeration, no multi-place edits).
42	- **Calibrants stay out of `rbw-ts` suites.** Their cases intentionally Fail/Skip to calibrate the harness, asserted under `rbw-tt` (`rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`). They are documented, not suited.
43	- **Navigational context is part of done.** Update the CLAUDE.md "Test Execution" section and `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-theurge-ifrit-context.md` so a restarted chat navigates the new model without rediscovery — including a definition of "calibrant" and the `rbw-tt` vs `rbw-ts` split.
44	
45	## Sources
46	
47	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — suite arrays + `rbte_suite` (→ forwarder)
48	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` — `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry + lookup
49	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdre_engine.rs` — `Fixture` struct + `case!` stringify precedent
50	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` — binary dispatch (add `suite` mode)
51	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` hand-mirror + `rbtdrm_required_colophons`
52	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` — calibrant meta-tests (held separate)
53	
54	## What done looks like
55	
56	`rbw-ts fast` runs the identical fixture set in identical order as today, and every suite name resolves to the membership it has now. No `ZRBTE_SUITE_*`/`zrbte_resolve_suite` in bash; no `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` strings anywhere. `rbw-tb` builds deny-warnings clean, `rbw-tt` green (calibrants included), `rbw-tq` clean. CLAUDE.md and the theurge context file describe the new test model and define "calibrant."

The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Co-maintained constant set in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earn a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. - **Fixture names are Rust-authored — out of the projection.** On inspection, fixtures are defined in Rust (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry; fixture structs name themselves), and bash holds only redundant copies in its `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` suite arrays — there is no canonical bash author, so fixtures fail the single-author rule. ## What done looks like

The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

# jjezs_pace ₢BPAAG

## Character

Intricate test-infrastructure refactor carrying a premise correction: the prior docket had ownership backwards. Rust authors fixtures (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry, fixture structs naming themselves); bash only holds redundant copies in its suite arrays. This is not a bash→Rust projection — it is a consolidation of test vocabulary into its rightful owner. Touches the spine of every `rbw-ts` run, so verification is behavioral, not just a green build.

## Goal

Make theurge (Rust) the sole owner of test vocabulary: relocate suite definitions from bash into theurge, and collapse fixture-name identity onto Rust identifiers — retiring every hand-maintained name string on both sides of the boundary.

## Shape

One end state ("Rust owns what fixtures exist, how they group, and what they're named"), reached along two strands:

- **Suites into theurge.** The `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays and `zrbte_resolve_suite` in `rbte_engine.sh` are the redundant copy of Rust-owned fixture names. Move suite definitions into a new Rust module (minted prefix `rbtdrs`, "suites"). A suite is compile-checked **fixture refs** (the statics `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` already holds), never name strings. `rbte_suite` collapses to: build binary, forward the suite name; the binary grows a `suite` dispatch mode beside its existing `single` mode. Each suite carries a human description + optional precondition **printed at runtime** at suite start, so a failure shows its context — superseding the buried bash precondition comments.

- **Fixture identity = Rust identifier.** Retire the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab consts; a fixture's name derives from its Rust identifier (the `case!` macro in `rbtdre_engine.rs` is the stringify precedent). Repoint every consumer (`grep RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`) and `rbtdrm_required_colophons` onto the derived names. Operator wire-names for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` shift kebab→identifier; accepted, since individual fixture runs are rare.

- **Zero new crates.** The suite registry and name derivation use native `macro_rules!` only — no `paste`, no concat crate. Derive names via `stringify!`; express composition as base-suite + extras concatenated at resolve time, so a base like `fast` stays single-source (no flat re-enumeration, no multi-place edits).
- **Calibrants stay out of `rbw-ts` suites.** Their cases intentionally Fail/Skip to calibrate the harness, asserted under `rbw-tt` (`rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`). They are documented, not suited.

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` — `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry + lookup

## What done looks like

`rbw-ts fast` runs the identical fixture set in identical order as today, and every suite name resolves to the membership it has now. No `ZRBTE_SUITE_*`/`zrbte_resolve_suite` in bash; no `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` strings anywhere. `rbw-tb` builds deny-warnings clean, `rbw-tt` green (calibrants included), `rbw-tq` clean. I've mounted ₣BP. Here's the orientation.

## Goal (₢BPAAG)

Make theurge (Rust) the **sole owner of test vocabulary** — relocate suite definitions out of bash into theurge, and collapse fixture-name identity onto Rust identifiers, retiring every hand-maintained name string on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary.

## Heat context

**rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup (₣BP)** — grab-bag cleanup: consolidate each capability/constant to its single rightful owner; remove anything redundantly held in two places. 5 of 8 paces done. The constant-projection thread (colophons, RBCC set) has already landed as `RBTDGC_*` generated consts (recent ₢BPAAC/D/E). This pace is the test-vocabulary strand, which the paddock explicitly carves *out* of the projection — fixtures have no canonical bash author, so the redundancy is removed in the other direction.

## Name assessment

The silks **`fixture-codegen-projection`** contradicts its own docket — the Character section opens with "this is **not** a bash→Rust projection." The name is a fossil from the superseded premise. I'd suggest renaming to something like `test-vocab-into-theurge` or `suites-and-fixture-names-to-rust`. Want me to relabel before we start?

## The docket (full)

**Two strands to one end state — "Rust owns what fixtures exist, how they group, and what they're named":**

1. **Suites into theurge.** `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays + `zrbte_resolve_suite` in `rbte_engine.sh` move into a new Rust module (minted prefix `rbtdrs`). A suite = compile-checked **fixture refs** (from `RBTDRC_FIXTURES`), never name strings. `rbte_suite` collapses to build-binary + forward-name; the binary grows a `suite` dispatch mode beside `single`. Each suite carries a human description + optional precondition **printed at runtime** at suite start.

2. **Fixture identity = Rust identifier.** Retire `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab consts; derive names from the Rust identifier (`case!` stringify precedent). Repoint every consumer + `rbtdrm_required_colophons`. `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` wire-names shift kebab→identifier (accepted — rare).

**Locked constraints:**
- **Zero new crates** — native `macro_rules!` + `stringify!` only; compose suites as base + extras concatenated at resolve time (so `fast` stays single-source).
- **Calibrants stay out of `rbw-ts` suites** — they intentionally Fail/Skip under `rbw-tt` (`rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`); documented, not suited.
- **Navigational context is part of done** — update CLAUDE.md "Test Execution" + `rbk-claude-theurge-ifrit-context.md`, defining "calibrant" and the `rbw-tt` vs `rbw-ts` split.

**Done:** `rbw-ts fast` runs identical fixtures in identical order; every suite resolves to today's membership; no `ZRBTE_SUITE_*`/`zrbte_resolve_suite`, no `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` strings anywhere; `rbw-tb` deny-warnings clean, `rbw-tt` green, `rbw-tq` clean; docs updated.

## Proposed approach

- **Read first**: `rbte_engine.sh` (suite arrays + resolver), `rbtdrc_crucible.rs` (`RBTDRC_FIXTURES`), `rbtdre_engine.rs` (`case!` precedent), `main.rs` (dispatch), `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` (fixture mirror + required-colophons) — to ground the macro design against what's actually there before writing.
- **Strand 2 likely lands first** (fixture-name derivation), since the new suite module in strand 1 references fixtures and benefits from identifier-derived names already existing.
- **Mint `rbtdrs`** for the suite registry; verify terminal-exclusivity against the `rbtd*` tree before committing the prefix.
- **Verification is behavioral**: a green build is necessary but not sufficient — must confirm `rbw-ts fast` produces the identical fixture set/order. Run `fast` (no deps), `rbw-tt`, `rbw-tq`.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential, opus-tier — architectural macro design with interleaved strands across ~6 Rust files + one bash forwarder; not parallelizable (the two strands share the fixture-reference surface).

Want me to relabel the silks first, and shall I proceed?

#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
    "canonical-invest"
    "dogfight"
  )
  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".

    "kludge-tadmor"
    "tadmor"
  )

}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

# zrbte_codegen() - Refresh all zipper-derived artifacts before compiling.
# Write-on-change, so unchanged files keep their mtime and cargo skips rebuilds.
# The build is the sole producer — there is no standalone generate command.
zrbte_codegen() {

    || buc_die "Failed to generate colophon consts"

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Building theurge"

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
    dogfight) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT[*]}" ;;
    tadmor)   echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR[*]}" ;;

  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen

  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"

  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}

# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE CLI - Theurge test engine CLI entry point
#
# Enrolled in rbz_zipper, dispatched by rbw_workbench via buz_exec_lookup.
# Public functions (rbte_build/test/run/suite/single) live in rbte_engine.sh;
# fixture/suite folio arrives via BUZ_FOLIO from the colophon channel.

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buym_yelp.sh"

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrbte_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TABTARGET_DIR    " "Tabtarget directory (dispatch-provided; codegen input)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TEMP_DIR         " "Temp directory (dispatch-provided; codegen scratch)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_cli_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_cli_dir}/../rbcc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_cli_dir}/../rbz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_cli_dir}/rbte_engine.sh"

  zbuv_kindle
  zburd_kindle
  zbuz_kindle
  zrbz_kindle
  zrbte_kindle
}

buc_execute rbte_ "Theurge test engine" zrbte_furnish "$@"

# eof

35	        RBTDGC_ROLE_GOVERNOR => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_GOVERNOR),
36	        RBTDGC_ROLE_RETRIEVER => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER),

50	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HALLMARK_LIFECYCLE: &str = "hallmark-lifecycle";
51	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_BATCH_VOUCH: &str = "batch-vouch";
52	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ACCESS_PROBE: &str = "access-probe";
53	// Fast fixtures (no external dependencies)
54	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION: &str = "enrollment-validation";

66	// retriever/director invest) against a depot the operator has already levied

81	// runnable artifact with NO crucible charged (the orthogonal axis skirmish
82	// covers). Ordains conjure-mode busybox, summons it, runs a degenerate
83	// container-runtime command proving executability, then abjures.

149	            RBTDGC_CHECK_GOVERNOR,
150	            RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER,
151	            RBTDGC_CHECK_DIRECTOR,
152	            RBTDGC_CHECK_PAYOR,
153	        ]),
154	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION
155	        | RBTDRM_FIXTURE_REGIME_VALIDATION

157	        | RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_VERDICTS

159	        | RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_PROGRESSING
160	        | RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_SENTINEL => Some(&[]),

162	            RBTDGC_HYGIENE_CHECK_DOCKERFILE,
163	            RBTDGC_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL,
164	        ]),
165	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE => Some(&[
166	            RBTDGC_LEVY_DEPOT,
167	            RBTDGC_LIST_DEPOT,
168	            RBTDGC_UNMAKE_DEPOT,
169	            RBTDGC_MANTLE_GOVERNOR,
170	            RBTDGC_INVEST_RETRIEVER,
171	            RBTDGC_INVEST_DIRECTOR,
172	            RBTDGC_DIVEST_RETRIEVER,
173	            RBTDGC_DIVEST_DIRECTOR,
174	            RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER,
175	            RBTDGC_CHECK_DIRECTOR,
176	        ]),
177	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_ESTABLISH => Some(&[
178	            RBTDGC_LEVY_DEPOT,
179	            RBTDGC_LIST_DEPOT,

183	            RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER,
184	            RBTDGC_CHECK_DIRECTOR,
185	        ]),
186	        // canonical-invest reuses canonical-establish's investiture cases sans
187	        // levy — same colophons minus LEVY_DEPOT (depot is operator-provided).
188	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_INVEST => Some(&[
189	            RBTDGC_LIST_DEPOT,
190	            RBTDGC_MANTLE_GOVERNOR,
191	            RBTDGC_INVEST_RETRIEVER,
192	            RBTDGC_INVEST_DIRECTOR,
193	            RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER,
194	            RBTDGC_CHECK_DIRECTOR,
195	        ]),
196	        // kludge-tadmor builds both vessels locally; only the two kludge
197	        // colophons are exercised (no charge/credential colophons here).
198	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_KLUDGE_TADMOR => Some(&[
199	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
200	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_KLUDGE_BOTTLE,
201	        ]),
202	        // dogfight ordains/summons/abjures a single conjure-mode hallmark; the
203	        // bare container-runtime run is shelled directly, not via a colophon.
204	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_DOGFIGHT => Some(&[
205	            RBTDGC_ORDAIN_HALLMARK,
206	            RBTDGC_SUMMON_HALLMARK,
207	            RBTDGC_ABJURE_HALLMARK,

211	            RBTDGC_YOKE_RELIQUARY,
212	            RBTDGC_ENSHRINE_VESSEL,
213	            RBTDGC_ORDAIN_HALLMARK,
214	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
215	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_KLUDGE_BOTTLE,
216	            RBTDGC_WREST_HALLMARK_IMAGE,
217	            RBTDGC_SUMMON_HALLMARK,
218	            RBTDGC_PLUMB_FULL,
219	            RBTDGC_PLUMB_COMPACT,
220	            RBTDGC_REKON_HALLMARK,
221	            RBTDGC_JETTISON_HALLMARK_IMAGE,
222	            RBTDGC_ABJURE_HALLMARK,
223	        ]),
224	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HANDBOOK_RENDER => Some(&[
225	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_START_HERE,
226	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_CRASH_COURSE,
227	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_CRED_RETRIEVER,
228	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_CRED_DIRECTOR,
229	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_FIRST_CRUCIBLE,
230	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_DIR_FIRST_BUILD,
231	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_PAYOR_HB,
232	            RBTDGC_ONBOARD_GOVERNOR_HB,
233	            RBTDGC_HANDBOOK_TOP,
234	            RBTDGC_HANDBOOK_WINDOWS,
235	            RBTDGC_HW_DOCKER_DESKTOP,
236	            RBTDGC_HW_DOCKER_CONTEXT,
237	            RBTDGC_PAYOR_ESTABLISH,
238	            RBTDGC_PAYOR_REFRESH,
239	            RBTDGC_QUOTA_BUILD,
240	        ]),
241	        _ => None,
242	    }
243	}
244	
245	/// Verify that all required colophons for a fixture appear in the zipper manifest string.
246	pub fn rbtdrm_verify(manifest: &str, fixture: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
247	    let required = match rbtdrm_required_colophons(fixture) {
248	        Some(r) => r,
249	        None => {
250	            return Err(format!(
251	                "rbtd: unknown fixture '{}' — not registered in manifest",
252	                fixture
253	            ));
254	        }
255	    };
256	    for colophon in required {
257	        let found = manifest.split_whitespace().any(|token| token == *colophon);
258	        if !found {
259	            return Err(format!(
260	                "rbtd: colophon '{}' not found in zipper manifest (fixture '{}')",
261	                colophon, fixture
262	            ));
263	        }
264	    }
265	    Ok(())
266	}

61	/// Sub-assertion within a case: run bash snippet, check exit code.
62	struct RbtdrfSub {
63	    label: &'static str,
64	    setup: &'static str,
65	    command: &'static str,
66	    expect_ok: bool,
67	}
68	
69	impl RbtdrfSub {
70	    const fn ok(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str) -> Self {
71	        Self { label, setup, command: "buv_vet \"TEST\"", expect_ok: true }
72	    }
73	    const fn fatal(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str) -> Self {
74	        Self { label, setup, command: "buv_vet \"TEST\"", expect_ok: false }
75	    }
76	    const fn ok_cmd(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str, command: &'static str) -> Self {
77	        Self { label, setup, command, expect_ok: true }
78	    }
79	    const fn fatal_cmd(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str, command: &'static str) -> Self {
80	        Self { label, setup, command, expect_ok: false }
81	    }
82	}
83	
84	/// Run a bash script, return (exit_code, stdout, stderr). Saves traces to case dir.
85	fn rbtdrf_run_bash(
86	    project_root: &Path,
87	    script: &str,
88	    dir: &Path,
89	    trace_prefix: &str,
90	) -> Result<(i32, String, String), String> {
91	    let output = Command::new("bash")
92	        .arg("-c")
93	        .arg(script)
94	        .current_dir(project_root)
95	        .output()
96	        .map_err(|e| format!("bash execution failed: {}", e))?;
97	
98	    let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).into_owned();
99	    let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).into_owned();
100	    let code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
101	
102	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-script.sh", trace_prefix)), script);
103	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stdout.txt", trace_prefix)), &stdout);
104	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stderr.txt", trace_prefix)), &stderr);
105	
106	    Ok((code, stdout, stderr))
107	}
108	
109	/// Run enrollment-validation sub-assertions against BUV.
110	fn rbtdrf_run_ev(
111	    dir: &Path,
112	    enrollment: &str,
113	    subs: &[RbtdrfSub],
114	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
115	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
116	        Ok(r) => r,
117	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
118	    };
119	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
120	
121	    for (i, sub) in subs.iter().enumerate() {
122	        let script = format!(
123	            "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n{}\n{}\n{}",
124	            rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
125	            enrollment,
126	            sub.setup,
127	            sub.command,
128	        );
129	
130	        match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, &format!("sub-{}", i)) {
131	            Ok((code, _, _)) => {
132	                let ok = code == 0;
133	                if ok != sub.expect_ok {
134	                    return if sub.expect_ok {
135	                        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", sub.label, code))
136	                    } else {
137	                        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected failure, got exit 0", sub.label))
138	                    };
139	                }
140	            }
141	            Err(e) => {
142	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", sub.label, e));
143	            }
144	        }
145	    }
146	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
147	}

149	/// Drive a regime's public `*_probate` function against a synthetic
150	/// baseline+override written to the case temp dir. `expect_ok` selects the
151	/// verdict polarity: pass-anchors validate the pristine baseline (true);
152	/// negatives apply one violating override and expect non-zero (false).
153	/// Sources only the contract surface — `buv_validation.sh` plus the one regime
154	/// module — mirroring the `buv_vet` bridge that enrollment-validation uses: no
155	/// internal-module chain, no z-private reach.
156	fn rbtdrf_run_probate(
157	    dir: &Path,
158	    module: &str,
159	    probate_fn: &str,
160	    baseline: &str,
161	    override_: &str,
162	    expect_ok: bool,
163	    label: &str,
164	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
165	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
166	        Ok(r) => r,
167	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
168	    };
169	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
170	    let module_path = root.join("Tools/rbk").join(module);
171	
172	    let env_file = dir.join(format!("{}.env", label));
173	    let body = format!("{}\n{}\n", baseline, override_);
174	    if let Err(e) = std::fs::write(&env_file, &body) {
175	        return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: write env failed: {}", label, e));
176	    }
177	
178	    let script = format!(
179	        "set -euo pipefail\n\
180	         source '{}'\n\
181	         source '{}'\n\
182	         zbuv_kindle\n\
183	         {} '{}'",
184	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
185	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&module_path),
186	        probate_fn,
187	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&env_file),
188	    );
189	
190	    match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, label) {
191	        Ok((code, _, _)) => {
192	            let ok = code == 0;
193	            if ok == expect_ok {
194	                rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
195	            } else if expect_ok {
196	                rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", label, code))
197	            } else {
198	                rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected failure, got exit 0", label))
199	            }
200	        }
201	        Err(e) => rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", label, e)),
202	    }
203	}

207	    project_root: &Path,
208	    colophon: &str,
209	    args: &[&str],
210	    dir: &Path,
211	    label: &str,
212	) -> Result<(), String> {
213	    let tt = rbtdri_find_tabtarget_global(project_root, colophon)?;
214	    let output = Command::new(&tt)
215	        .args(args)
216	        .current_dir(project_root)
217	        .output()
218	        .map_err(|e| format!("{}: failed to run {}: {}", label, tt.display(), e))?;
219	
220	    let code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
221	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stdout.txt", label)), &output.stdout);
222	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stderr.txt", label)), &output.stderr);
223	
224	    if code != 0 {
225	        return Err(format!(
226	            "{}: {} exited {} — {}",
227	            label,
228	            colophon,
229	            code,
230	            String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr),
231	        ));
232	    }
233	    Ok(())
234	}

238	/// (tabtarget not found, launcher failure) still propagate as Err.

240	    project_root: &Path,

520	// --- Reference types ---
521	
522	const RBTDRF_VALID_DIGEST: &str =
523	    "sha256:abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789";
524	
525	fn rbtdrf_ev_odref_valid(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
526	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
527	        Ok(r) => r,
528	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
529	    };
530	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
531	    let enrollment = format!(
532	        "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n\
533	         buv_regime_enroll \"TEST\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"References\"\n\
534	         buv_odref_enroll TEST_IMAGE \"Container image\"",
535	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
536	    );
537	    let d = RBTDRF_VALID_DIGEST;
538	
539	    let subs: &[(&str, &str)] = &[
540	        ("standard registry", &format!("docker.io/library/alpine@{}", d)),
541	        ("multi-level repo", &format!("us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-proj/my-repo/tool@{}", d)),
542	        ("registry with port", &format!("registry.local:5000/myimage@{}", d)),
543	    ];
544	
545	    for (i, (label, image)) in subs.iter().enumerate() {
546	        let script = format!("{}\nexport TEST_IMAGE=\"{}\"\nbuv_vet \"TEST\"", enrollment, image);
547	        match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, &format!("sub-{}", i)) {
548	            Ok((0, _, _)) => {}
549	            Ok((code, _, _)) => {
550	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", label, code));
551	            }
552	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", label, e)),
553	        }
554	    }
555	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
556	}

1	## Theurge & Ifrit: Crucible Security Testing
2	
3	Read this file when working on theurge (test orchestrator), ifrit (attack binary), or crucible test cases. This is Recipe Bottle's internal test infrastructure only — Recipe Bottle does not require users to have Rust compilation tools.
4	
5	### Architecture
6	
7	Two Rust binaries with completely different roles and build targets:
8	
9	- **Theurge** (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/`) — test orchestrator, runs on the **host** (macOS/Linux). Charges a crucible (sentry + pentacle + bottle containers), invokes attacks, observes results, produces verdicts. Built via `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh`.
10	- **Ifrit** (`rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/`) — attack binary, runs **inside the bottle container**. Probes network security boundaries from the attacker's perspective. Source lives in the shared build context consumed by `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` (and the forthcoming airgap variant). Built inside the Docker image during `docker build` — there is no host-side compilation, no cross-compile, no `cargo check` on macOS. The Dockerfile IS the build system.
11	
12	**Coordinated tests** are the distinctive capability: theurge simultaneously observes from outside (via sentry writ/fiat commands) while ifrit attacks from inside. Neither binary alone can do this.
13	
14	### Crucible Iteration Loop
15	
16	The typical development cycle when changing ifrit or theurge code:
17	
18	#### Iteration strategy: single cases first, full suite last
19	
20	The full tadmor fixture takes ~10 minutes (charge + 50+ cases + quench). **Do not run it as the first verification step** — whether debugging failures or verifying new code. Instead:
21	
22	1. Charge the crucible once
23	2. Run single-case against each new or changed test to verify it passes
24	3. Run the full fixture only after all targeted cases pass — as a final regression check
25	4. Quench
26	
27	This applies to **all** crucible verification: new tests, bug fixes, refactors. The full suite is always the last step, never the first.
28	
29	#### Full run (charge + all cases + quench in one command)
30	
31	1. Edit ifrit source (`rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/`) or theurge source (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`)
32	2. If ifrit changed: kludge-rebuild the bottle image
33	   ```
34	   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor
35	   ```
36	   This builds a new container image and drives the kludge hallmark into the nameplate's `rbrn.env`.
37	3. Git commit the hallmark change (kludge dirties `rbrn.env` — a clean working tree is required for charge, and the commit trail maintains audit integrity)
38	4. Run the full tadmor fixture:
39	   ```
40	   tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh tadmor
41	   ```
42	   This charges the crucible, runs all cases, and quenches — one command.
43	
44	#### Single-case debugging (manual charge/quench lifecycle)
45	
46	When iterating on a specific failing test case:
47	
48	1. Charge the crucible (starts containers, leaves them running):
49	   ```
50	   tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
51	   ```
52	2. Run individual cases against the live crucible:
53	   ```
54	   tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh tadmor case-name
55	   ```
56	   Omit the case name to list all available cases. Omit the fixture name to list all available fixtures.
57	3. Edit code, rebuild as needed (kludge for ifrit, `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` for theurge), re-run the single case. Repeat.
58	4. Quench when done:
59	   ```
60	   tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh
61	   ```
62	
63	#### Ordaining after green
64	
65	Kludge builds are for rapid local iteration. Once all tests pass with the kludge hallmark, ordain for a production-grade Cloud Build image:
66	
67	1. Ordain the vessel:
68	   ```
69	   tt/rbw-fO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether
70	   ```
71	2. Summon the ordained hallmark locally:
72	   ```
73	   tt/rbw-fs.RetrieverSummonsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether <hallmark>
74	   ```
75	3. Drive the ordained hallmark into the nameplate (edit `RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK` in `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env`), commit, and re-run the full fixture to verify.
76	
77	**Hallmark prefixes** tell you what you have: `k` = kludge (local build), `c` = conjured (Cloud Build ordained).
78	
79	### Test Cases
80	
81	Bottle/sentry security cases — shared verbatim by the `tadmor` and `moriah` fixtures (`RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`). The two fixtures differ only in provenance: tadmor runs against a locally-kludged bottle, moriah against the cloud-built airgap bottle. Runtime semantics are identical.
82	
83	Cases group by purpose in source order (no formal section structure):
84	
85	- **basic-infra** — smoke tests: pentacle ping, dnsmasq responds
86	- **ifrit-attacks** — single ifrit attack, verdict from inside only: dns-allowed/blocked, apt blocked
87	- **observation** — sentry-side observation of bottle behavior: iptables loaded, blocked-with-observation
88	- **correlated** — theurge resolves on sentry, ifrit attacks with result: tcp443 allow/block, ICMP hop tests
89	- **sortie-attacks** — multi-step ifrit sorties: DNS exfil, metadata probe, raw socket smuggle
90	- **unilateral-novel** — ifrit sorties testing novel attack vectors: route manipulation, subnet escape, DNAT reflection
91	- **coordinated-attacks** — simultaneous attack + observation: ARP gratuitous/poison, table stability
92	- **coordinated-integrity** — attack then verify sentry state unchanged: sentry integrity, DNS cache integrity, MAC flood
93	
94	### Adding a New Test
95	
96	**New ifrit attack** (simple probe, single command):
97	1. Add constant, enum variant, `from_selector`/`selector`/`all_selectors` entries in `rbida_attacks.rs`
98	2. Add dispatch arm in `rbida_run()`
99	3. Add theurge case function calling `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "selector-name", dir)`
100	4. Register in `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` (or the relevant fixture's case array)
101	
102	**New ifrit sortie** (complex multi-step attack):
103	1. Add `pub fn sortie_name()` in `rbida_sorties.rs`

## Theurge & Ifrit: Crucible Security Testing

Read this file when working on theurge (test orchestrator), ifrit (attack binary), or crucible test cases. This is Recipe Bottle's internal test infrastructure only — Recipe Bottle does not require users to have Rust compilation tools.

### Architecture

Two Rust binaries with completely different roles and build targets:

- **Theurge** (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/`) — test orchestrator, runs on the **host** (macOS/Linux). Charges a crucible (sentry + pentacle + bottle containers), invokes attacks, observes results, produces verdicts. Built via `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh`.
- **Ifrit** (`rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/`) — attack binary, runs **inside the bottle container**. Probes network security boundaries from the attacker's perspective. Source lives in the shared build context consumed by `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` (and the forthcoming airgap variant). Built inside the Docker image during `docker build` — there is no host-side compilation, no cross-compile, no `cargo check` on macOS. The Dockerfile IS the build system.

**Coordinated tests** are the distinctive capability: theurge simultaneously observes from outside (via sentry writ/fiat commands) while ifrit attacks from inside. Neither binary alone can do this.

### Crucible Iteration Loop

The typical development cycle when changing ifrit or theurge code:

#### Iteration strategy: single cases first, full suite last

The full tadmor fixture takes ~10 minutes (charge + 50+ cases + quench). **Do not run it as the first verification step** — whether debugging failures or verifying new code. Instead:

1. Charge the crucible once
2. Run single-case against each new or changed test to verify it passes
3. Run the full fixture only after all targeted cases pass — as a final regression check
4. Quench

This applies to **all** crucible verification: new tests, bug fixes, refactors. The full suite is always the last step, never the first.

#### Full run (charge + all cases + quench in one command)

1. Edit ifrit source (`rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/`) or theurge source (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`)
2. If ifrit changed: kludge-rebuild the bottle image
   ```
   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor
   ```
   This builds a new container image and drives the kludge hallmark into the nameplate's `rbrn.env`.
3. Git commit the hallmark change (kludge dirties `rbrn.env` — a clean working tree is required for charge, and the commit trail maintains audit integrity)
4. Run the full tadmor fixture:
   ```
   tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh tadmor
   ```
   This charges the crucible, runs all cases, and quenches — one command.

#### Single-case debugging (manual charge/quench lifecycle)

When iterating on a specific failing test case:

1. Charge the crucible (starts containers, leaves them running):
   ```
   tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
   ```
2. Run individual cases against the live crucible:
   ```
   tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh tadmor case-name
   ```
   Omit the case name to list all available cases. Omit the fixture name to list all available fixtures.
3. Edit code, rebuild as needed (kludge for ifrit, `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` for theurge), re-run the single case. Repeat.
4. Quench when done:
   ```
   tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh
   ```

#### Ordaining after green

Kludge builds are for rapid local iteration. Once all tests pass with the kludge hallmark, ordain for a production-grade Cloud Build image:

1. Ordain the vessel:
   ```
   tt/rbw-fO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether
   ```
2. Summon the ordained hallmark locally:
   ```
   tt/rbw-fs.RetrieverSummonsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether <hallmark>
   ```
3. Drive the ordained hallmark into the nameplate (edit `RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK` in `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env`), commit, and re-run the full fixture to verify.

**Hallmark prefixes** tell you what you have: `k` = kludge (local build), `c` = conjured (Cloud Build ordained).

### Test Cases

Bottle/sentry security cases — shared verbatim by the `tadmor` and `moriah` fixtures (`RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`). The two fixtures differ only in provenance: tadmor runs against a locally-kludged bottle, moriah against the cloud-built airgap bottle. Runtime semantics are identical.

Cases group by purpose in source order (no formal section structure):

- **basic-infra** — smoke tests: pentacle ping, dnsmasq responds
- **ifrit-attacks** — single ifrit attack, verdict from inside only: dns-allowed/blocked, apt blocked
- **observation** — sentry-side observation of bottle behavior: iptables loaded, blocked-with-observation
- **correlated** — theurge resolves on sentry, ifrit attacks with result: tcp443 allow/block, ICMP hop tests
- **sortie-attacks** — multi-step ifrit sorties: DNS exfil, metadata probe, raw socket smuggle
- **unilateral-novel** — ifrit sorties testing novel attack vectors: route manipulation, subnet escape, DNAT reflection
- **coordinated-attacks** — simultaneous attack + observation: ARP gratuitous/poison, table stability
- **coordinated-integrity** — attack then verify sentry state unchanged: sentry integrity, DNS cache integrity, MAC flood

### Adding a New Test

**New ifrit attack** (simple probe, single command):
1. Add constant, enum variant, `from_selector`/`selector`/`all_selectors` entries in `rbida_attacks.rs`
2. Add dispatch arm in `rbida_run()`
3. Add theurge case function calling `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "selector-name", dir)`
4. Register in `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` (or the relevant fixture's case array)

**New ifrit sortie** (complex multi-step attack):
1. Add `pub fn sortie_name()` in `rbida_sorties.rs`
2. Add constant, enum variant, and dispatch in `rbida_attacks.rs` (same as above)
3. Add theurge case — sorties may need coordinated observation (writ/fiat before/after)
4. Register in `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` (or the relevant fixture's case array)

**Crucible verification workflow** (applies to all new tests and changes):

1. Build theurge: `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` and run unit tests: `tt/rbw-tt.Test.sh`
2. If ifrit source changed: kludge-rebuild, commit hallmark, then charge:
   ```
   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor    # builds image, drives hallmark into rbrn.env
   # commit the rbrn.env change (clean tree required for charge)
   tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
   ```
3. Verify each new/changed case individually against the live crucible:
   ```
   tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh tadmor rbtdrc_sortie_new_case_name
   ```
4. Only after all targeted cases pass, run the full fixture for regression:
   ```
   tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh tadmor
   ```
   Note: this charges and quenches internally — quench the manual crucible first if one is active (`tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh`).

Study complete. I have the full picture and won't start work. Here's what I found.

## The two redundancies, concretely

**Strand 1 — Suites (bash holds the redundant copy).** `rbte_engine.sh:42-164` defines 8 `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays + `zrbte_resolve_suite`, listing fixture **name strings**. `rbte_suite` builds the binary then loops, invoking it once per fixture (`rbte_engine.sh:201-221`). The suite-name list is also echoed in `rbz_zipper.sh:204` (enroll description) and in the `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.{name}.sh` filenames.

**Strand 2 — Fixture names (dual bash↔Rust).** `rbtdrm_manifest.rs:45-90` holds 21 `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab consts. Each fixture static's `name:` field references one (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2293+`, `rbtdrf_fast.rs:1737+`, plus rbtdrk/rbtdro/rbtdrp/rbtdrd/rbtdrl). The registry `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2358`) + `rbtdrc_lookup_fixture` resolve by `f.name`. The `case!` macro (`rbtdre_engine.rs:136`) is the `stringify!` precedent.

## Key safety confirmation

`rbtdri_invocation.rs:78-83` documents that for crucible fixtures, **the fixture name IS the nameplate moniker** (drives `rbw-cC.Charge.{fixture}.sh`). Those four — `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml` — plus `dogfight` are single tokens, already valid Rust identifiers, so kebab→identifier derivation leaves them **unchanged**. No charge/quench tabtarget renames. Only multi-word names shift (e.g. `enrollment-validation` → `enrollment_validation`), which only affects rare `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` operator typing — exactly what the docket accepts.

## Blast radius (smaller than the silks implied)

- **Bash functional:** only `rbte_engine.sh` (arrays/resolver → forwarder) and `rbz_zipper.sh:204` (description string). Two stale *comments* at `rbob_cli.sh:164` and `rbq_Qualify.sh:212` mention changing names — non-load-bearing.
- **No bash calibrant driver exists** — the "bash blackbox testbench" in `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`'s comments has zero bash consumers of those names (verified). Calibrant rename stays inside Rust.
- **Rust:** new `rbtdrs_suites.rs` + `lib.rs` reg; `main.rs` gains a `suite` mode; `rbtdre_engine.rs` (macro/struct); every fixture static; `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`; tests rbtdtm/rbtdtl/rbtdti/rbtdtk/rbtdto.
- **Docs (part of done):** `CLAUDE.md` Test Execution, `Tools/buk/claude-buk-core.md`, `rbk-claude-theurge-ifrit-context.md`. **Not** the memos / retired `jjh_*` (frozen records).
- The silks was doubly wrong: there is **no codegen** in this pace (suites/names become Rust-native via `macro_rules!`, not generated files). The existing diff-gate at `rbq_Qualify.sh:154-166` belongs to the *other* projection paces, untouched here.

## The one design fork worth your call

`rbtdrm_required_colophons` (`rbtdrm_manifest.rs:122`) is a `match` on the fixture name. Rust match arms need **const patterns** — today those are the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` consts. Once names derive from a macro/`stringify!`, the match can't key on them, and replacing arms with literal strings would just re-introduce hand-maintained name strings (violating "no name strings anywhere").

My recommendation: **fold `required_colophons` into the `rbtdre_Fixture` struct** (each fixture declares its own, shared sets via named `&[&str]` statics referencing `RBTDGC_*`). Then `rbtdrm_required_colophons` collapses to `lookup(name).map(|f| f.required_colophons)` and the giant match disappears. This is the cleanest "consolidate to the single owner" move and the only shape I see that fully satisfies the docket's "no name strings" + "repoint required_colophons" without `paste`. The alternative (keep the match, derive arms some other way) fights the zero-crate constraint. I'd want your nod on this before building, since it touches the struct.

Two behavioral-parity notes I'll preserve: the suite loop moving into one Rust process must keep **cross-fixture fail-fast** (today the bash `set -e` for-loop; gauntlet/skirmish depend on it), and the **tree-clean guard runs once at suite start**, not per fixture.

Since you may pause: want me to capture this study (the design fork + blast radius) into the ₢BPAAG docket or the ₣BP paddock so a resumed session doesn't re-derive it? Otherwise I'll hold here and start when you return.

# jjezs_reslate BPAAG

## Character

Intricate test-infrastructure refactor, premise-corrected: Rust authors fixtures; bash only mirrors their names in suite arrays. This is an ownership relocation, NOT a bash→Rust projection — there is no codegen and no diff-gate here (those belong to the sibling projection paces). Study has de-risked it: the bash surface is tiny, the work is almost entirely Rust, and the one design fork is now locked. Verification is behavioral, not just a green build — this touches the spine of every `rbw-ts` run.

## Goal

Make theurge (Rust) the sole owner of test vocabulary: relocate suite definitions into theurge, derive fixture names from Rust identifiers, and retire both the bash suite arrays and the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` const family.

## Locked design

- **Fixture name = Rust identifier.** Add a `fixture!` `macro_rules!` deriving `name` via `stringify!` of an identifier token — the `case!` macro in `rbtdre_engine.rs` is the exact precedent. No `paste`, no concat crate.
- **Nameplate monikers stay verbatim.** `rbtdri_invocation.rs` documents that crucible fixture names ARE nameplate monikers (they drive `rbw-cC.Charge.{name}.sh`). `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`, `dogfight` are single tokens already — their derived names MUST equal today's strings. Only multi-word kebab names become underscore identifiers; that wire-name shift for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` is accepted.
- **`required_colophons` folds into the `rbtdre_Fixture` struct** (operator-accepted). Each fixture declares its own; shared sets are named `&'static [&'static str]` statics referenced by multiple fixtures (the crucible four, the empty fast/calibrant group, etc. — preserve today's groupings as single-source). `rbtdrm_required_colophons` collapses to a registry lookup returning the field; `rbtdrm_verify` keeps its signature and behavior. Delete the entire `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` family.
- **Suites move to a new Rust module, minted prefix `rbtdrs`.** A suite is compile-checked fixture refs (`&'static rbtdre_Fixture`), never name strings. Express composition as base-suite + extras concatenated at resolve time (runtime Vec chaining), so a base like `fast` stays single-source. Each suite carries a human description + optional precondition, PRINTED at runtime at suite start (superseding the buried bash precondition comments).
- **Binary gains a `suite` dispatch mode** beside `single`. It resolves the suite, prints description/precondition, runs the tree-clean guard ONCE at suite start, then loops fixtures (per-fixture verify + setup/charge + cases + teardown/quench), stopping at the first fixture that reports failures — preserving the cross-fixture fail-fast the bash `set -e` loop gives gauntlet/skirmish today.
- **`rbte_suite` (bash) collapses** to: build binary, forward the suite name to the binary's `suite` mode. Delete `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` and `zrbte_resolve_suite`; update the suite-name list in the `RBZ_THEURGE_SUITE` enroll description in `rbz_zipper.sh`.

## Out of scope / leave alone

- Operation-verb, container-role, and module/probate consts in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` are NOT fixture names — leave them. `rbtdrm_credential_check_colophon` (role→probe) untouched.
- Calibrants are never added to a `rbw-ts` suite — they intentionally Fail/Skip and are asserted only under `rbw-tt`. Document, don't suite.
- No generated file, no `rbq` diff-gate added. Do not edit memos or retired `jjh_*` records.

## Discovery (sites drift — use these, don't enumerate)

- `grep -rn RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ Tools/rbk/rbtd/src` — every consumer to repoint: source fixture statics (`name:` field → macro) and test lookups/asserts.

## Character

Intricate test-infrastructure refactor, premise-corrected: Rust authors fixtures; bash only mirrors their names in suite arrays. This is an ownership relocation, NOT a bash→Rust projection — there is no codegen and no diff-gate here (those belong to the sibling projection paces). Study has de-risked it: the bash surface is tiny, the work is almost entirely Rust, and the one design fork is now locked. Verification is behavioral, not just a green build — this touches the spine of every `rbw-ts` run.

## Goal

Make theurge (Rust) the sole owner of test vocabulary: relocate suite definitions into theurge, derive fixture names from Rust identifiers, and retire both the bash suite arrays and the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` const family.

## Locked design

- **Fixture name = Rust identifier.** Add a `fixture!` `macro_rules!` deriving `name` via `stringify!` of an identifier token — the `case!` macro in `rbtdre_engine.rs` is the exact precedent. No `paste`, no concat crate.
- **Nameplate monikers stay verbatim.** `rbtdri_invocation.rs` documents that crucible fixture names ARE nameplate monikers (they drive `rbw-cC.Charge.{name}.sh`). `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`, `dogfight` are single tokens already — their derived names MUST equal today's strings. Only multi-word kebab names become underscore identifiers; that wire-name shift for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` is accepted.
- **`required_colophons` folds into the `rbtdre_Fixture` struct** (operator-accepted). Each suite carries a human description + optional precondition, PRINTED at runtime at suite start (superseding the buried bash precondition comments).
